nativeRange.habitat: Clear lagoons and seaward reefs with coral-rich areas, particularly around Acropora; depth range 1–30 m

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on macroalgae and filamentous algae; adults have been documented also consuming tunicates and sponges. Offer dried seaweed sheets (nori) via a lettuce clip, along with Gracilaria and caulerpa. Supplement with herbivore-formulated pellets. A well-fed specimen tends to be less likely to nip at corals.

summary: A vividly patterned Indo-Pacific rabbitfish with a distinctive yellow-striped face and blue-spotted body. Generally peaceful and reef-safe when well fed; bears venomous dorsal spines requiring careful handling.
careNotes: The Decorated Rabbitfish is generally considered one of the more rewarding rabbitfish to keep, though it requires a spacious aquarium — LiveAquaria recommends a minimum of 180 gallons (approximately 681 L) given its active swimming habits and adult size of roughly 28 cm (11 in) in captivity. Wild specimens have been documented reaching up to 38 cm total length (FishBase), though aquarium individuals tend to remain smaller. Juveniles have been known to school around Acropora corals in the wild; adults typically occur in pairs. The species is generally considered reef-safe when well fed, but has been reported to nip at LPS and soft corals if undernourished — providing ample herbivore foods (nori, Gracilaria, caulerpa) is strongly advised. IMPORTANT: All dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous and can inflict a painful sting; handle only with a net, never with bare hands. The species tends to adopt a mottled camouflage coloration when stressed or sleeping — this is a normal stress/rest response and not a sign of illness. Adults tend to do best kept singly in the home aquarium, though mated pairs have been maintained in larger systems. As with other Siganidae, avoid housing with conspecifics or other rabbitfish species unless the tank is exceptionally large. Temperature note: FishBase habitat models indicate a wild range of 22–27°C; aquarium trade sources typically recommend 72–78°F (22–25.6°C). The combined range (22–27°C) covers both perspectives; optimal aquarium temperature is generally advised at 24–26°C. Diet note: FishBase records adults consuming tunicates and sponges in addition to algae (trophic level 3.0), suggesting occasional omnivorous tendencies, though algae should form the dietary core.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reliably documented for this species. In the wild, adults tend to form pair bonds and are pelagic broadcast spawners. Replication of the necessary environmental cues (lunar, tidal) makes captive breeding essentially infeasible in home aquaria.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 38 cm TL wild, common 25 cm), temperature range (22–27°C), native distribution (Indo-West Pacific: Cocos-Keeling Islands and South China Sea to Gilbert Islands, north to Ryukyu Islands, south to southern Great Barrier Reef and New Caledonia), taxonomy (family Siganidae, order Acanthuriformes), and diet (algae, tunicates, sponges; trophic level 3.0). FishBase max size (38 cm) is notably larger than LiveAquaria's cited captive size (11 in / 27.9 cm); both figures recorded, with captive range (20–28 cm) used for adultSizeCm. Preferred habitat temperature modeled at 24.7–29°C on FishBase vs trade sources' 72–78°F (22–25.6°C); combined range 22–27°C used.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner; no reliable captive breeding documented. Wild adults form pair bonds and respond to lunar/tidal cues.
